meta.title: How a Graph Neural Network Sees a Node
meta.subtitle: An explorable publication about message passing, receptive fields, and oversmoothing.
meta.note: The essay uses one scalar feature channel to make message passing visible. Real GNNs usually propagate vectors.

scenes.name: Homophily Signal
scenes.title: One layer is enough when neighbors already agree
scenes.dek: The target starts uncertain, but its immediate neighborhood is dominated by orange evidence.
scenes.task: Predict whether the selected node belongs to the orange community.
scenes.depth_prompt: This motif is solved by a one-layer GNN because the decisive evidence is one edge away.

stage_notes.features: At depth 0 the target only knows its own feature. A value near 0.50 is almost neutral, so an MLP-like baseline hesitates.
stage_notes.messages: Each incoming edge scales the sender state. Strong orange neighbors send large positive messages into the target.
stage_notes.aggregate: The neighborhood summary is a weighted average, not a raw sum. That keeps degree from dominating the update by itself.
stage_notes.update: The target keeps part of its own feature but mostly follows the neighborhood summary, which pushes it into the orange class.
stage_notes.depth: A second layer barely changes the answer. Once the first hop is informative, deeper propagation mostly smooths the graph.

depth_notes.0: Depth 0 is MLP-like: the target only sees its own ambiguous feature.
depth_notes.1: After one layer the target absorbs strong orange evidence from its neighbors.
depth_notes.2: The answer is already stable. Additional depth mostly smooths nearby nodes together.
depth_notes.4: At four layers the graph is noticeably smoother, but the target remains orange.
depth_notes.6: The graph is still separable, yet the states are less contrasted than they were at shallow depth.

scenes.name: Two-Hop Witness
scenes.title: Two layers reveal evidence that is hidden two hops away
scenes.dek: The target listens to two relay nodes. Those relays only become informative after they have listened to their own neighborhoods.
scenes.task: Predict whether the selected node should turn orange once two-hop context is included.
scenes.depth_prompt: This motif shows why stacking message-passing layers expands the receptive field.

stage_notes.features: The target again starts almost neutral. Looking only at its own feature is not enough to classify it confidently.
stage_notes.messages: At the first step the target only hears from the relay nodes below the true signal. Their messages are still weak.
stage_notes.aggregate: The first-hop aggregate is close to neutral because the relays have not yet incorporated the upstream orange sources.
stage_notes.update: After one layer the relays are prepared, but the target is still uncertain. The useful evidence is one step further out.
stage_notes.depth: After two layers the target can finally receive the upstream signal through the relays. This is the core advantage of stacked GNN layers.

depth_notes.0: Depth 0 sees only the target feature, so the prediction is nearly a coin flip.
depth_notes.1: After one layer the target has only listened to the relays, which are still close to neutral.
depth_notes.2: After two layers the orange sources have reached the target through the relay nodes.
depth_notes.4: More layers keep amplifying the same motif, but they also start smoothing the entire graph.
depth_notes.6: At six layers the answer is very confident, but the graph is much less locally distinct than it was at depth two.

scenes.name: Bridge Graph
scenes.title: Extra depth can wash out a boundary
scenes.dek: Two communities are connected by a bridge node. Repeated averaging makes both sides drift toward the same state.
scenes.task: Track how community contrast shrinks as message passing is applied again and again.
scenes.depth_prompt: This motif is not about missing information. It is about too much mixing.

stage_notes.features: The two communities start far apart, with the bridge node exactly in the middle.
stage_notes.messages: Messages flow across the bridge in both directions. Local smoothing now becomes global mixing.
stage_notes.aggregate: Because the bridge sees both communities, its aggregate stays almost neutral while its neighbors get pulled inward.
stage_notes.update: Every update shrinks the contrast between the left and right halves of the graph.
stage_notes.depth: At larger depth the graph becomes smoother and smoother. Distinctions do not disappear instantly, but they keep compressing.

depth_notes.0: At depth 0 the orange and blue communities are sharply separated.
depth_notes.1: One round of averaging mostly smooths within each community.
depth_notes.2: By two layers the bridge has started to pull both sides inward.
depth_notes.4: At four layers the two communities are still separable, but the gap has already narrowed substantially.
depth_notes.6: At six layers the graph is much flatter. This is the start of oversmoothing: node states are becoming too similar.
